description: This figure presents a comparative scatter plot of specific hardness (hardness divided by density, HV·cm³/g) versus density (g/cm³) for the developed Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels against various reference material classes. The x-axis spans 6.0–9.0 g/cm³ and the y-axis spans 0–160 HV·cm³/g. Three alloy variants from this study—15Al (blue filled circles), 17Al (green filled circles), and 19Al (red filled circles)—are prominently clustered at low densities (~6.6–6.9 g/cm³) with ultra-high specific hardness values exceeding 120 HV·cm³/g and reaching above 140 HV·cm³/g. These clusters sit well above a dashed reference line near 120 HV·cm³/g. The background includes comparative data clouds for conventional low-density steels (gray-shaded region with orange and blue open circles), other steels including stainless and carbon steels (pink open squares, ~7.3–8.0 g/cm³), pure iron (black triangle near 7.8 g/cm³), and medium/high-entropy alloys M/HEAs (blue open hexagons, ~7.5–8.8 g/cm³). The plot visually establishes that the Fe-Mn-Al-Cu alloys simultaneously achieve lower density and markedly higher specific hardness than existing steels and M/HEAs, supporting the core conclusion that these alloys overcome the specific hardness ceiling of conventional high-performance steels.
